➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Lengthy sitting consumes qi, and overwork damages tendons and muscles. Trauma such as a sprain causes stagnation of qi and blood, leading to pain. Aggravation of pain when tired is because of qi exhaustion. The tendons and muscles are deprived of nourish ment from blood stasis, so there is numbness in the fingers. Dark tongue with purple spots and hesitant pulse are signs of blood stasis.

[b]➣ c) Liver and kidney deficiency[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] In old age the constitution is weak, qi and blood are deficient, the tendons and bones are poorly nourished, resulting in pain in the neck, shoulder and arm, and numbness and weakness in the four extremities. The lumbar area is said to be the dwelling place of the kidneys, and functions to store essence and support the Thoroughfare and Conception vessels. When kidney essence is insufficient, it causes lumbar debility and soreness, sper- matorrhea and irregular menstruation. Kidney yin deficiency with hyperactive liver yang gives rise to dizziness and tinnitus. Red tongue, little coating, thin and weak pulse are signs of liver and kidney deficiency.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Houxi (SI 3) and Tianzhu (BL 10) are the points of Hand- and Foot-Taiyang meridians, which pass through the neck. They are selected according to the principle that where the meridian goes the related disease can be treated with its points. Tianzhu (BL 10) is a local point. The distal point Houxi (SI 3) is confluent with the Governor Vessel. The combination of the local and distal points removes the obstruction of Taiyang Meridian and Governor Vessel, and stops pain. Dazhui (GV 14) is the point where all the yang meridians meet. Acupuncture at Dazhui (GV 14) can activate the qi of all the yang meridians to remove obstructions. Cervical Jiaji (Extra) regulates qi and blood in the local region, and stops pain.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Neck pain, cervical spondylosis, is a disease manifested as pain and numbness of the neck region referring to the head, shoulder and upper extremity, and sometimes accompanied by dizziness. It is usually caused by regressive degeneration and osteophyte of cervical inter- vertebral discs pressing the spinal cord, nerve root, vessels and the sympathetic nerve in the local region.","Patterns":"➣ Working at a desk for a long time with the head bent forward makes the neck muscles tired, leading to disturbance of local circulation of qi in the meridians, especially in the Governor Vessel. Invasion of pathogenic wind cold and damp can result in obstruction of Taiyang meridians. In old age with qi and blood deficiency the tendons and muscles lack nourishment and the bones are weak due to deficient kidney essence producing little marrow for bone support.","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Wind cold obstruction[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Neck pain and stiffness, soreness of shoulder and arm, motor impairment of neck, numbness and cold sensation of arm and hand which is aggravated when exposed to cold, sometimes accompanied with cold extremities and soreness of the whole body, thin white or white sticky tongue coating, string-taut and tense pulse.

[b]➣ b) Blood stasis due to overexertion[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Neck pain referring to the upper back and upper arm, numbness of fingers aggravated by fatigue. Dark tongue with purple spots, hesitant pulse.

[b]➣ c) Liver and kidney deficiency[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Pain in neck, shoulder and arm, numbness and weakness in the four extremities, dizziness, tinnitus, lumbar debility and soreness, spermatorrhea, irregular menstruation. Red tongue, little coating, thin and weak pulse.","Treatment Principle":"➣ [b]Method:[/b] Relax the tendons and muscles, activate the blood circulation, dispel wind and cold, dissolve damp, and reinforce the liver and kidneys. Points are mainly selected from the Hand- and Foot-Taiyang meridians and Foot-Shaoyang Meridian. Local points and distal points of the affected meridians are used in combination. Acupuncture with reinforc ing method is adopted for liver and kidney deficiency, while acupuncture with reducing method is applied for wind-cold obstruction and blood stasis due to overexertion.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Dazhui (GV 14), Tianzhu (BL 10), Houxi (SI 3), Cervical Jiaji (Extra).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b]

➣ [b]Wind cold obstruction:[/b] Fengmen (BL 12), Fengfu (GV 16).

➣ [b]Blood stasis due to overexertion:[/b] Geshu (BL 17), Hegu (LI 4), Taichong (LR 3).

➣ [b]Liver and kidney deficiency:[/b] Ganshu (BL 18), Shenshu (BL 23), Zusanli (ST 36).

➣ [b]Local points:[/b] Jianjing (GB 21), Tianzong (SI 11).

➣ [b]Pain and numbness in arm and fingers:[/b] Hegu (LI 4), Quchi (LI 11), Waiguan (TE 5).

➣ [b]Dizziness, headache and blurred vision:[/b] Baihui (GV20), Fengchi (GB 20), Taiyang (Extra).

➣ [b]Nausea and vomiting:[/b] Tiantu (CV 22), Nei- guan (PC 6).","Remarks":"➣ Acupuncture improves the symptoms of cervical spondylosis significantly, and it is especially effective for the cervical vertebral type and vertebral artery type. Tuina-massage and traction are applicable in combination.

➣ [b]Appendix:[/b] Torticollis

➣ Torticllis here refers to wry neck caused by an awkward sleeping posture or attack of wind cold on the nape that leads to disturbance of local circulation of qi in the meridians. Its main manifestations are stiffness and pain of the neck and nape, and wry neck toward one side with motor impairment. [b]Method:[/b] Points of the Governor Vessel and Taiyang Meridians are selected as the principal points. The reducing meth od and moxibustion are applied to Dazhui (GV 14), Tianzhu (BL 10), Jianwaishu (SI 14), Xuanzhong (GB 39), Houxi (SI 3) to expel wind and disperse cold, and to relax tendons and activate blood and qi circulation in the meridians. Kun lun (BL 60) and Lieque (LU 7) are add ed for inability of flexion and extension. Zhizeng (SI 7) is added for difficulty in rotating the neck so as to promote the qi circulation of Taiyang Meridians. Cup ping may be applied after needling, or Laozhen (Extra) is used alone for stiff neck.","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Neck Pain","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Pain in Hypochondriac Region","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Excess type[/b]

[b]➣ i) Stagnation of qi[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Distending pain in the coastal and hypochondriac region suggests obstruction of the collaterals due to failure of the liver in maintaining the free flow of qi. Severity of the symptoms varies with the emotional state because of the close relationship between emotional changes and stagnation of qi. Stifling sensation in the chest and sighing indicate uneven qi activity. Poor appetite shows that the spleen is being attacked by the liver qi. Thin white tongue coating and string-taut pulse are the signs of depression of the liver.

[b]➣ ii) Stagnation of blood:[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Fixed, stabbing pain in the hypochondriac region is caused by stagnation of blood following stagnation of qi in the hypochondriac region. Pain intensified at night suggests that blood as a yin factor is apt to stagnate at night which is the yin time of a day. Pain due to stagnation of blood is a condition of excess, so it is aggravated by pressure. Dark, purplish tongue proper, and deep, hesitant pulse are signs of blood stagnation.

[b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Dull pain in the costal and hypochondriac region indicates deficiency of essence and blood, which causes poor nourishment of the collaterals of the liver. Dryness of the mouth and irritability suggests deficiency of yin with endogenous heat. Dizziness and blurring of vision are due to the shortage of essence and blood. Red tongue with little coating, and weak or rapid and thready pulse are signs of deficiency of essence and blood with endogenous heat.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"[b]➣ a) Excess type[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] The Shaoyang Meridian supplies the lateral aspect of the body; so Zhigou (TE 6) and Yanglingquan (GB 34) are used to relieve pain by regulating the qi of the Shao- yang Meridian. Qimen (LR 14), the Front-Mu point of the Liver Meridian, eases the liver and relieves pain in the hypochondrium. Taichong (LR 3) and Qiuxu (GB 40) regulate the qi of the liver and gallbladder. Geshu (BL 17) and Ganshu (BL 18) can activate blood circulation and remove stasis.

[b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]


➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Ganshu (BL 18), the Back- (Shu) point of the liver, Shenshu (BL 23), the Back-Shu point of the kidney, Qimen (LR 14), the Front-Mu point of the liver, Taichong (LR 3), the Yuan-Primary point of the Liver Meridian, used in combination, can nourish essence and blood, readjust the liver and relieve pain. Zusanli (ST 36) and Sanyinjiao (SP 6) strengthen the function of the spleen and stomach, which are the main sources of producing qi and blood.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Hypochondriac pain is a subjective symptom commonly seen in the clinic. It may be unilateral or bilateral. [b]The classic book Miraculous Pivot points out:[/b] "Pathogenic factors in the liver give rise to hypochondriac pain." [b]The twenty-second chapter of Plain Questions says:[/b] "When the liver is diseased, it causes pain below the ribs on both sides, and then refers to the lower abdomen." As the meridian of the liver supplies the hypochondriac regions, and the liver is externally and internally related with the gallbladder, the occurrence of hypochondriac pain is mostly concerned with disorders of the liver and gallbladder.","Patterns":"➣[b] a) The liver is situated in the hypochondriac region. Its meridians supply bilateral hypochondriac regions. If it is diseased, it will cause hypochondriac pain. The liver is the organ in the category of wind and wood of Five Elements, and prefers to be in a harmonious state with free flow of qi. Emotional depression may restrain the liver function, causing poor circulation of qi in the meridians, often resulting in hypochondriac pain.[/b]

➣[b] b) The prolonged stagnation of the liver qi, or traumatic injuries such as sprain and contusion may cause stasis of blood in collaterals, resulting in hypochondriac pain.[/b]

➣[b] c) Poor health associated with chronic disease, overstrain and stress, may cause deficiency of essence and blood, which in turn produces poor nourishment of the liver and its collaterals, resulting in hypochondriac pain.[/b]","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Excess type[/b]

[b]➣ i) Stagnation of qi[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Distending pain in the costal and hypochondriac region, stifling sensation in the chest, sighing, poor appetite, bitter taste in the mouth, thin white tongue coating, string-taut pulse. Severity of the symptoms varies with the changes of emotional state.

[b]➣ ii) Stagnation of blood:[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Fixed, stabbing pain in the hypochondriac region, intensified by pressure and at night, dark purplish tongue proper, deep and hesitant pulse.

[b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Dull pain lingering in the costal and hypochondriac region, dryness of the mouth, irritability, dizziness, blurring of vision, red tongue with little coating, weak, or rapid and thready pulse.","Treatment Principle":"[b]➣ a) Excess type[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Points are mainly selected from Jueyin and Shaoyang Meridians of Foot to remove the stagnation of liver qi and the obstruction in the collaterals. Needling with reducing method is to be applied.

[b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Nourish essence and blood, invigorate circulation of qi, and relieve pain by applying reinforcing method to points of the Foot-Jueyin Meridian and Back-Shu points.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b] a) Excess type[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Qimen (LR 14), Zhigou (TE 6), Yanglingquan (GB 34).

➣ [b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Qimen (LR 14), Ganshu (BL 18), Shenshu (BL 23); Zusanli (ST 36), Sanyin- jiao (SP 6), Taichong (LR 3).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b] a) Excess type[/b]

➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b]

➣ [b]Stagnation of qi:[/b] Taichong (LR 3), Qiuxu (GB 40).

➣ [b]Stagnation of blood:[/b] Geshu (BL 17), Ganshu(BL 18).

➣ [b]➣ b) Deficiency type[/b]","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) Hypochondriac pain is seen in diseases of the liver and gallbladder, contusion of the hypochondriac region, inter-costal neuralgia and costal chondritis.[/b]

[b]➣ b) Application of Huatuojiaji points of the corresponding segments gives gratifying effect to relieve pain in the treatment of intercostal neuralgia.[/b]

[b]➣ c) Dermal needle therapy: Tap the skin over the affected hypochondriac area, and then apply cupping. This method is indicated in hypochondriac pain due to sprain or contusion. It has the action of removing stasis and relieving pain.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Pain in Hypochondriac Region","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Lower Pack Pain","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Cold damp[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Pathogenic cold and damp characterized by viscosity and stagnation block the meridians and collaterals, causing retarded circulation of qi and blood. This produces heaviness, cold sensation and pain in the lumbar region and limitation of extension and flexion of the back. Stagnation of qi and blood becomes worse on cloudy and rainy days, and so does the pain. Accumulation of cold and damp gives rise to the white, sticky tongue coating and the deep, weak or deep, slow pulse.

[b]➣ b) Kidney deficiency[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] The lumbar region is said to be the "dwelling house of the kidney." The kidney dominates the bones, produces marrow and stores essence. When the kidney has insufficient essence, the bone is lacking in marrow, and the result is soreness and pain in the lumbar region accompanied by weakness of the knees. Over strain and stress consume essence and qi, and make the pain worse. Pain is lessened by bed rest, which makes qi quiescent. In case of deficiency of kidney yang, the kidney fails to warm the lower abdomen and the limbs. This gives rise to cramp-like sensation in the lower abdo- men and cold limbs. Deficiency of yang causes pallor, pale tongue, deep-thready or deep-slow pulse. When yin is deficient, kidney water is unable to ascend to reduce the heart fire. This results in irritability and insomnia. [b]Deficiency of yin causes excessive internal heat, which gives rise to the following symptoms:[/b] flushed face, feverish sensation in the chest, palms and soles, dryness of the mouth and throat, reddened tongue with little coating, thready weak or thready rapid pulse.

[b]➣ c) Trauma[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Muscular strain in the lumbar region causes retardation of qi and blood and further leads to stagnation of blood in the meridians and collaterals. The result is the fixed, severe pain which can be aggravated by pressure. String-taut pulse is associated with pain, dark purplish tongue and hesitant pulse are signs of blood stasis.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] The lower back is the "dwelling house of the kidney." Shenshu (BL 23) can be selected to tonify the qi of the kidney. Moxibustion may also be applied to this point to eliminate cold and damp. Yaoyangguan (GV 3) is a local point. Weizhong (BL 40) is one of "Four Key Points," and an important distal point for the treatment of low back pain. Dachangshu (BL 25) and Guanyuanshu (BL 26) can dispel wind and cold, remove obstruction in meridians, and relieve pain. Combination of acupuncture and moxibustion applied to Mingmen (GV 4) and Yaoyang (Extra) can tonify the kidney yang and strengthen the Kidney essence as well. Zhishi (BL 52) and Taixi (KI 3) are selected for the purpose of nourish ing the kidney yin. As the Governor Vessel travels along the spine, Renzhong (GV 26) is a distal point effective for treating rigidity and pain of the lumbar region. Yaotongxue (Extra) is an empirical point used in treating sprain of the lumbar region.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Lower back pain (pain in the lumbar region) is closely associated with disorders of the kidney for the lumbar area is the seat of the kidney.

➣ Clinically, lower back pain can be found in various diseases. [b]This section only deals with the following etiological factors:[/b] 1. Invasion of exogenous pathogenic cold and damp; 2. Deficiency of qi of the kidney; and 3. Sprain or contusion.","Patterns":"➣[b] a) Invasion by pathogenic cold and damp: In this case lower back pain is due to obstruction of circulation of qi in meridians and collaterals, The precipitating factors may be living in cold and damp places, exposure to the rain or wading in water, or being drenched with sweat.[/b]

➣[b] b) Deficiency of the kidney qi: In this case lower back pain is generally due to excessive sexual activity that consumes essence and qi, resulting in poor nourishment of the meridians in the lumbar region.[/b]

➣[b] c) Trauma due to sprain or contusion: Trauma may cause injury of qi and blood in the meridians and collaterals, leading to stagnation of qi and blood, thus producing lower back pain.[/b]","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Cold damp[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Lower back pain usually occurring after exposure to cold and damp and aggravated on rainy days, heavy sensation and stiffness of the muscles in the dorsolum- bar region, limitation of extension and flexion of the back, pain radiating downwards to the buttocks and lower limbs, cold feeling of the affected area, white and sticky tongue coating, deep and weak, or deep and slow pulse.

[b]➣ b) Kidney deficiency[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Insidious onset of protracted pain and soreness, accompanied by lassitude and weakness of the lumbar region and knees, aggravated by fatigue and alleviated by bed rest. In case of deficiency of kidney yang, cramp-like sensation in the lower abdomen, pallor, normal taste in the mouth, cold limbs, pale tongue, deep-thready or deep-slow pulse. In case of deficiency of kidney yin, irritability, insomnia, dry mouth and throat, flushed face, feverish sensation in the chest, palms and soles, reddened tongue proper with scanty coating, thready-weak or thready-rapid pulse.

[b]➣ c) Trauma[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] History of sprain of the lumbar region, rigidity and pain of the lower back which is generally fixed in a certain area, and is aggravated by pressure and by turning the body, pink or dark purplish tongue, string-taut, hesitant pulse.","Treatment Principle":"➣ [b]Method:[/b] Points are mainly selected from the Governor Vessel and the Foot-Taiyang Meridian to promote the circulation of qi and blood, to relieve pain, to relax the muscles and to activate the blood circulation in the collaterals. Acupuncture and moxibustion are applied together for cold-damp type. In case of deficiency of the kidney yang, apply needling with reinforcing method and moxibustion. For deficiency of the kidney yin, puncture with reinforcing method. For traumatic low back pain, apply reducing method or pricking to cause bleeding,","Point Prescription":"➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Shenshu (BL 23), Yaoyang- guan (GV 3), Weizhong (BL 40).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b]

➣ [b]Cold damp:[/b] Dachangshu (BL 25), Guan- yuanshu (BL 26).

➣ [b]Deficiency of the kidney yang:[/b] Mingmen (GV 4), Yaoyan (Extra).

➣ [b]Deficiency of the kidney yin:[/b] Zhishi (BL 52), Taixi (KI 3).

➣ [b]Traumatic injury:[/b] Renzhong (GV 26), Yao- tongxue (Extra), Ashi points.","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) Lower back pain may be seen in renal diseases, rheumatism, rheumatoid arthritis, hyperplastic spondilities, muscle strain or traumatic injury of the lumbar region.[/b]

[b]➣ b) When the lumbar vertebrae are diseased, the corresponding Huatuojiaji points may be punctured perpendicularly 1.0-1.5 inches. Needles are retained. Here, acupuncture therapy is only a supplementary method of treatment.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Lower Pack Pain","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Bi Syndromes","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Wandering bi[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Pain in the joints is a common manifestation of all the bi syndromes caused by wind, cold and damp that obstruct qi and blood circulation in meridians and collater- als. As stated in Chinese medicine, "There is pain if there is obstruction." Wandering pain is due mainly to invasion by pathogenic wind which is characterized by constant movement and changes. Chills and fever result from the struggle between anti-pathogenic factors and pathogenic factors after the invasion. Superficial tense or superficial slow pulse indicates invasion of exogenous pathogenic wind on the exterior of body; and thin and sticky tongue coating shows the initial stage of invasion by pathogenic wind, cold and damp.

[b]➣ b) Painful bi[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Severe pain is due to retarded circulation of qi and blood in the meridians and collaterals caused by excessive cold. Cold is a yin pathogenic factor, characterized by causing contraction. The pain is localized because of the congealing effect of cold. Pain alleviated by warmth suggests that heat improves the circulation of blood. Cold causes further stagnation of blood, and hence aggravates the pain. The absence of local redness and hotness is characteristic of affection by pathogenic cold. String-taut and tense pulse is associated with cold and pain. White tongue coating is a sign of pathogenic cold.

[b]➣ c) Fixed bi[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Pathogenic damp is characterized by heaviness. When it is in an excessive state, it invades the limbs and joints, causing retarded

➣ circulation of qi and blood, and resulting in numbness and heaviness. Pathogenic damp is a yin factor, characterized by viscosity and stagnation. So the pain caused by damp is also fixed in location. The condition becomes worse on cloudy and rainy days for the weather change brings about more stagnation of qi and blood. Soft pulse and white, sticky tongue coating indicate the presence of pathogenic damp.

[b]➣ d) Heat bi[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Local redness, swelling and pain of the joints are the result of the transformation of pathogenic factors into heat. Movement is limited because of swelling and deformity of joints. Fever, thirst, yellow tongue coating, rolling and rapid pulse are the signs of excessive heat. [b]In addition, bi syndromes may also be classified according to the locality of the diseased area as follows:[/b] [b]Skin bi:[/b] Numbness of the skin with cold sensation. [b]Muscle bi:[/b] Soreness, numbness and pain of the muscles. [b]Tendon bi:[/b] Soreness, pain and stiffness of the tendons and muscles. [b]Vessel bi:[/b] Pain due to blockage of vessels. [b]Bone bi:[/b] Soreness, heaviness and pain of joints which fail to perform their functions of lifting, extension and flexion.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"➣ Ahshi points together with the local and distal points along the yang meridians supplying the diseased areas are selected for the purpose of eliminating wind, cold and damp. Wandering bi, heat bi and tendon bi are mainly treated by the reducing method. Subcutaneous needles may also be applied. For painful bi and vessel bi, it is better to use moxibustion, and apply needling as an adjuvant treatment with deep insertion and prolonged retaining of the needles. For severe pain, intradermal needles or indirect moxibustion with ginger may be used. Fixed bi, skin bi, muscle bi and bone bi may also be treated by combined acupuncture and moxibustion, or together with warming needle, or tapping plus cupping,

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] The above prescriptions are formulated by selection of the local and distal points on the meridians supplying the diseased areas. The principle of the treatment is to remove obstruction from the meridians and collaterals and to regulate ying (nutrient qi) and wei (defensive qi) for elimination of wind, cold and damp. When the skin and muscles are dis- eased, shallow insertion should be used. When bones and tendons are affected, deep insertion with retaining of the needles is recommended. The methods of acupuncture and moxibustion depend on symptoms and signs. Houxi (SI 3) communicates with the Governor Vessel, and Shenmai (BL 62) with the Yangqiao Meridian. They are a set of the Eight Confluent points for the treatment of diseases of the shoulder, back, lumbar region, legs, muscles, tendons and bones. Dabao (SP 21) is the major Luo- (Connecting) point of the spleen which con- nects qi of the whole body, and Geshu (BL 17) is the Influential point of blood. Combination of these two points can be used to treat general pain. Dazhui (GV 14) and Quchi (LI 11) are used to treat heat bi. Geshu (BL 17) and Xuehai (SP 10) have the function of activating and nourishing the blood. [b]The selection is based on the principle:[/b] "Wind will be naturally eliminated if blood circulates smoothly." Fengchi (GB 20), the most important point for dispelling wind, can be combined with Geshu (BL 17) and Xuehai (SP 10) to treat wandering bi and vessel bi. Shangqiu (SP 5) and Zusanli (ST 36) strengthen the function of spleen and stomach and eliminate damp to relieve the fixed bi. Guanyuan (CV 4) and Shenshu (BL 23) strengthen the kidney fire and relieve the painful bi. Yanglingquan (GB 34), the Influential Point of the tendon, is used to treat the tendon bi. Dazhu (BL 11), the Influential Point of the bone, Xuanzhong (GB 39), the Influential Point of the marrow, can be used together in treating the bone bi.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Bi syndromes are the syndromes character ized by obstruction of qi and blood in merid ians and collaterals due to invasion of pathogenic wind, cold and damp, and manifested by soreness, pain, numbness and heavy sensation of the limbs and joints, and limitation of movement.

➣ Clinically, bi syndromes are common in the areas where the weather is cold, wet and windy, occurring in persons of either sex and any age. In mild cases there are only soreness and pain in limbs and joints, aggravated by the change of weather. In severe cases the soreness and pain are marked and recur repeatedly, accompanied by swelling of the joints and even deformity and limitation of movement.

➣ [b]Bi syndromes may be classified into four types according to etiology and manifestations:[/b] 1) Wandering bi is characterized by migrating pain and caused chiefly by pathogenic wind; 2) Painful bi is characterized by severe pain and

➣ caused chiefly by pathogenic cold; 3) Fixed bi is characterized by marked soreness, numbness and heaviness and caused chiefly by pathogenic damp; and 4) Heat bi is characterized by heat manifestations and sudden onset.","Patterns":"➣[b] a) Attack of pathogenic factors in individuals with weakened body resistance: Bi syndromes are caused by obstruction of qi and blood due to 1) invasion of the meridians and collaterals by pathogenic wind, cold and damp; 2) general weakness of the body with deficiency of yang qi; and 3) dysfunction of the pores, and weakness of defensive yang. The book Recipes for Saving Lives points out, "It is because of weakness of the body with poor function of the pores that invasion of wind, cold and damp to produce bi syndromes is possible."[/b]

➣[b] b) Body constitution: The body constitution differs in the natures of heat and cold. In case of body constitution with exuberant yang qi and accumulated heat, invasion of pathogenic wind, cold and damp will give rise to heat bi. Furthermore, long-standing wind, cold and damp bi syndromes may turn into heat bi as the pathogenic factors in the meridians and collaterals are transformed into heat.[/b]","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Wandering bi[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Wandering pain in the joints, especially the wrists, elbows, knees and ankles; limitation of movement, chills and fever, thin and sticky tongue coating, superficial and tight or superficial and slow pulse.

[b]➣ b) Painful bi[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Severe stabbing pain in the joints, alleviated by warmth and aggravated by cold, with fixed localization but no local redness and hotness, thin and white tongue coating, string-taut and tense pulse.

[b]➣ c) Fixed bi[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Numbness and heavy sensation of the limbs, soreness and fixed pain of the joints, aggravated on cloudy and rainy days, white and sticky tongue coating and soft pulse.

➣ circulation of qi and blood, and resulting in numbness and heaviness. Pathogenic damp is a yin factor, characterized by viscosity and stagnation. So the pain caused by damp is also fixed in location. The condition becomes worse on cloudy and rainy days for the weather change brings about more stagnation of qi and blood. Soft pulse and white, sticky tongue coating indicate the presence of pathogenic damp.

[b]➣ d) Heat bi[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Arthralgia involving one or several joints, local redness, swelling and excruciating pain with limitation of movement, accompanied by fever and thirst, yellow tongue coating, rolling and rapid pulse.","Treatment Principle":"➣ Ahshi points together with the local and distal points along the yang meridians supplying the diseased areas are selected for the purpose of eliminating wind, cold and damp. Wandering bi, heat bi and tendon bi are mainly treated by the reducing method. Subcutaneous needles may also be applied. For painful bi and vessel bi, it is better to use moxibustion, and apply needling as an adjuvant treatment with deep insertion and prolonged retaining of the needles. For severe pain, intradermal needles or indirect moxibustion with ginger may be used. Fixed bi, skin bi, muscle bi and bone bi may also be treated by combined acupuncture and moxibustion, or together with warming needle, or tapping plus cupping,","Point Prescription":"➣ Ahshi points together with the local and distal points along the yang meridians supplying the diseased areas are selected for the purpose of eliminating wind, cold and damp. Wandering bi, heat bi and tendon bi are mainly treated by the reducing method. Subcutaneous needles may also be applied. For painful bi and vessel bi, it is better to use moxibustion, and apply needling as an adjuvant treatment with deep insertion and prolonged retaining of the needles. For severe pain, intradermal needles or indirect moxibustion with ginger may be used. Fixed bi, skin bi, muscle bi and bone bi may also be treated by combined acupuncture and moxibustion, or together with warming needle, or tapping plus cupping,

➣ [b]Prescriptions:[/b]

➣ [b]Pain in the shoulder joint:[/b] Jianyu (LI 15), Jian- liao (TE 14), Jianzhen (TE 19), Naoshu (SI 10).

➣ [b]Pain in the scapula:[/b] Tianzong (SI 11), Bing- feng (SI 12), Jianwaishu (SI 14), Gaohuang (BL 43).

➣ [b]Pain in the elbow:[/b] Quchi (LI 11), Chize (LU 5), Tianjing (TE 10), Waiguan (TE 5), Hegu (LI 4).

➣ [b]Pain in the wrist:[/b] Yangchi (TE 4), Yangxi (LI 5), Yanggu (SI 5), Waiguan (TE 5).

➣ [b]Stiffness of the fingers:[/b] Yanggu (SI 5), Hegu (LI 4), Houxi (SI 3).

➣ [b]Numbness and pain in the fingers:[/b] Houxi (SI 3), Sanjian (LI 3), Baxie (Extra).

➣ [b]Pain in the lumbar region:[/b] Renzhong (GV 26), Shenzhu (GV 12), Yaoyangguan (GV 3).

➣ [b]Pain in the hip joint:[/b] Huantiao (GB 30), Ju- liao (GB 29), Xuanzhong (GB 39).

➣ [b]Pain in the thigh region:[/b] Zhibian (BL 54), Chengfu (BL 36), Yanglingquan (GB 34).

➣ [b]Pain in the knee joint:[/b] Heding (Extra), Dubi (ST 35), Medial Xiyan (Extra), Yanglingquan (GB 34), Yinlingquan (SP 9).

➣ [b]Numbness and pain in the leg:[/b] Chengshan (BL 57), Feiyang (BL 58).

➣ [b]Pain in the ankle:[/b] Jiexi (ST 41), Shangqiu (SP 5), Qiuxu (GB 40), Kunlun (BL 60), Taixi (KI 3).

➣ [b]Numbness and pain in the toes:[/b] Gongsun (SP 4), Shugu (BL 65), Bafeng (Extra).

➣ [b]Pain in the back:[/b] Shuigou (GV 26), Shen- zhu (GV 12), Yaoyangguan (GV 3).

➣ [b]General pain:[/b] Houxi (SI 3), Shenmai (BL 62), Dabao (SP 21), Geshu (BL 17), Jianyu (LI 15), Quchi (LI 11), Hegu (LI 4), Yangchi (TE 4), Huantiao (GB 30), Yanglingquan (GB 34), Xuanzhong (GB 39), Jiexi (ST 41).","Supplementary Points":"➣ Ahshi points together with the local and distal points along the yang meridians supplying the diseased areas are selected for the purpose of eliminating wind, cold and damp. Wandering bi, heat bi and tendon bi are mainly treated by the reducing method. Subcutaneous needles may also be applied. For painful bi and vessel bi, it is better to use moxibustion, and apply needling as an adjuvant treatment with deep insertion and prolonged retaining of the needles. For severe pain, intradermal needles or indirect moxibustion with ginger may be used. Fixed bi, skin bi, muscle bi and bone bi may also be treated by combined acupuncture and moxibustion, or together with warming needle, or tapping plus cupping,

➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b] 1.

➣ [b]Wandering bi, vessel bi:[/b] Geshu (BL 17), Xuehai (SP 10); 2.

➣ [b]Painful bi:[/b] Shenshu (BL 23), Guanyuan (CV 4); 3.

➣ [b]Fixed bi:[/b] Zusanli (ST 36), Shangqiu (SP 5); 4.

➣ [b]Heat bi:[/b] Dazhui (GV 14), Quchi (LI 11); 5.

➣ [b]Tendon bi:[/b] Yanglingquan (GB 34); and 6.

➣ [b]Bone bi:[/b] Dazhu (BL 11), Xuanzhong (GB 39).","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) Bi syndromes may include such diseases as rheumatic fever, rheumatic arthritis, theu- matoid arthritis, fibrositis, neuralgia and gout.[/b]

[b]➣ b) Dermal needle and cupping therapies: Heavy tapping to induce slight bleeding along the two sides of the spine or the local area of the affected joint plus cupping is often used for the treatment of skin bi and muscle bi associated with numbness, and bone bi characterized by stiffness and limitation of movement or deformity of the joint.[/b]

[b]➣ c) Acupuncture is effective in treating mild bi syndromes. For severe cases, a long period of treatment is necessary. In chronic cases with exhaustion of ying (nutrient qi) and wei (defensive qi) and undernourishment of tendons and muscles, the bi syndrome may turn into a wei syndrome.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Bi Syndromes","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Wei Syndromes","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Heat in the lung[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Fever and cough are the results of invasion of the lung by pathogenic heat. Irritability, thirst and scanty, brownish urine indicate that the body fluid has been damaged by internal heat. Muscular flaccidity and motor impairment result from malnutrition of the tendons and muscles and damage of essence and body fluid. The thready, rapid pulse and red tongue with yellow coating indicate that the body fluid has been injured by heat. The rolling, rapid pulse is associated with excessive heat.

[b]➣ b) Damp heat[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Flaccidity of the legs is due to the stagnation of qi and blood in the tendons and muscles caused by prolonged accumulation of internal damp-heat. General heaviness is also due to accumulation of damp-heat. When damp-heat is accumulated in the chest, fullness sensation in the chest and epigastrium results. Hot, brownish urine, and painful urination suggest the downward flow of damp heat. Yellow sticky tongue coating, and soft rapid pulse are signs of dampheat.

[b]➣ c) Deficiency of yin of the liver and kidney[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] In deficiency of yin of the liver and kidney the muscles, tendons and bones are poorly nourished by essence and blood, and muscular flaccidity with motor impairment occurs. Soreness and weakness of the lumbar region, seminal emission and leukorrhoea are the result of deficiency of essence in the kidney. Since the kidney is located in the lumbar region, it stores essence, and its meridians connect with the Thoroughfare Vessel and the Conception Vessel. Dizziness and blurring of vision are caused by preponderance of yang in the liver arising from deficiency of yin in the kidney. Red tongue and thready and rapid pulse are signs of deficiency of yin of the liver and kidney.

[b]➣ d) Trauma[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Flaccid paralytic limbs arise from obstruction of the circulation of qi and blood at the injured site of trauma. Incontinence of urine and feces is mainly due to dysfunction of the kidney which fails to control urine and feces. In case of trauma, the Governor Vessel which dominates the yang qi of the whole body is affected, and the qi activity of all zang-fu organs may be impaired, including the function of the kidney in controlling urine and feces. Damage of qi of the kidney causes incontinence of urine and feces. Hesitant pulse and dark purplish tongue indicate blood stasis.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] In the above prescription ing. [b]This is based upon the statement in Internal points the Yangming Meridians are predominat- Classic:[/b] "Only points along the Yangming Meridians are selected to treat paralysis of the limbs." Yanglingquan (GB 34) and Xuanzhong (GB 39), the Influential Points of tendon and marrow respectively, are added to enhance the effect of nourishing the tendons and bones. Feishu (BL 13) and Chize (LU 5) are used to dissipate heat from the lung. Pishu (BL 20) and Yinlingquan (SP 9) eliminate damp heat. Ganshu (BL 18) and Shenshu (BL 23) are chosen to tonify the yin in the liver and kidney. Huatuojiaji points are selected to regulate qi in the Governor Vessel. Zhongji (CV 3) and Sanyinjiao (SP 6) are taken to adjust the qi in the kidney and bladder. Dachangshu (BL 25) and Ciliao (BL 32) improve the function of the large intestine.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ The wei syndrome is characterized by flaccid- ity or atrophy of the limbs with motor impairment. It is also called "flaccid lame," for the leg is usually involved. The wei syndrome was first described in the forty-fourth chapter of Plain Questions as a syndrome mainly caused by heat in the lung with the lobes scorched. The physicians of later generations further developed this theory. Zhang Jingyue (1156-1228) [b]pointed out:[/b] "It is not a few cases of wei syndromes that are due to the injury of primary qi leading to deficiency of essence which fails to irrigate, or deficiency of blood which fails to nourish." [b]On the treatment of wei syndromes, the forty-fourth chapter of Plain Questions puts forward the theory:[/b] "Only points along the Yangming Meridians are selected in treating wei syndromes." The stomach is believed to be the sea of water and food, and the source of acquired essence. The Foot-Yangming Meridian is enriched with qi and blood. The twelve meridians, tendons, bones, and muscles need the acquired qi and blood for nourishment, while the production of blood in the liver and essence in the kidney depends upon the transformation of water and food. Therefore, regulating the function of the Stomach Meridian of Yangming is the main principle in treating the syndromes. In clinical practice, treatment is determined according to differentiation of the syndrome as well as locality, etiology and patho- genesis of the disease. In a chronic bi syndrome there may be prolonged motor impairment of the joint because of pain. In this case muscular wei atrophy or flaccidity of the limb develops because of disuse. It should be differentiated from the wei syndrome which is characterized by ab- sence of pain.","Patterns":"➣[b] a) Burning heat in the lung: The muscular flaccidity or atrophy of the limb results from mal nourishment of the tendons due to exhaustion of body fluid. This condition may be caused by invasion of the lung by exogenous pathogenic heat, or excessive heat remaining in the lung after an illness.[/b]

➣[b] b) Damp beat: Exogenous pathogenic damp invades in the body, and the accumulation of damp is eventually transformed into heat, which damages the muscles and tendons. Hence, the muscles and tendons become flaccid. The wei syndromes may also be caused by excessive intake of greasy food, which produces internal accumulation of damp heat, resulting in the stagnation of qi and blood in the meridians and collaterals.[/b]

➣[b] c) Deficiency of yin in the liver and kidney: Since the liver stores blood and controls the tendons, and the kidney stores essence and dominates the bones, prolonged illness or indulgent sexual activity causes loss of essence and blood, resulting in malnutrition of the tendons. Conditions affecting the proper function of the liver and kidney may therefore also give rise to the wei syndrome.[/b]

➣[b] d) Trauma: Contusion causes injury of the Meridians and leads to retarded flow of qi and blood in the meridians. As a result, the muscles and tendons are poorly nourished, and become flaccid. Thus the wei syndrome occurs.[/b]","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Heat in the lung[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Muscular flaccidity of the lower limbs with motor impairment, accompanied by fever, cough, irritability, thirst, scanty and brownish urine, reddened tongue with yellow coating, thready and rapid or rolling and rapid pulse.

[b]➣ b) Damp heat[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Flaccid or slight swollen legs, slight hot sensation on touch, general heaviness, sensation of fullness in the chest and epigastric region, painful urination, hot and brownish urine, yellow sticky tongue coating, soft and rapid pulse.

[b]➣ c) Deficiency of yin of the liver and kidney[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Muscular flaccidity of the lower limbs with motor impairment, combined with soreness and weakness of the lumbar region, seminal emission, prospermia, leukorrhoea, dizziness, blurring of vision, red tongue, thready and rapid pulse.

[b]➣ d) Trauma[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] History of trauma, flaccid paralytic limbs, may be accompanied with incontinence of urine and feces, relaxed or hesitant pulse, pink or dark purplish tongue with thin white coating.","Treatment Principle":"➣ [b]Method:[/b] Main points are selected from the Yangming Meridians to promote circulation of qi in the meridians, and to nourish the tendons and bones. If heat or damp heat in the lung is the main etiological factor, the reducing method should be used to dissipate heat. In case of deficiency of yin in the liver and kidney, the reinforcing method should be employed. For trauma, puncture the points on the affected side with even movement method.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b]Prescription:[/b]

➣ [b]Upper limb:[/b] Jianyu (LI 15), Quchi (LI 11), Hegu (LI 4), Waiguan (TE 5).

➣ [b]Lower limb:[/b] Biguan (ST 31), Huantiao (GB 30), Xuchai (SP 10), Liangqiu (ST 34), Zusanli (ST 36), Yanglingquan (GB 34), Jiexi (ST 41), Xuanzhong (GB 39).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b]

➣ [b]Heat in the lung:[/b] Chize (LU 5), Feishu (BL13).

➣ [b]Damp heat:[/b] Pishu (BL 20), Yinlingquan (SP9).

➣ [b]Deficiency of yin in the liver and kidney:[/b] Ganshu (BL 18), Shenshu (BL 23).

➣ [b]Trauma:[/b] Huatuojiaji points at the corresponding level of spinal injury.

➣ [b]Incontinence of urine:[/b] Zhongji (CV 3), Sanyinjiao (SP 6).

➣ [b]Incontinence of feces:[/b] Dachangshu (BL 25), Ciliao(BL 32).","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) The wei syndrome is seen in acute my- elitis, progressive myatrophy, myathenia gravis, multiple neuritis, sequellae of poliomyelitis, periodic paralysis, hysterical paralysis, traumatic paraplegia, etc.[/b]

[b]➣ b) Since the wei syndrome needs a long period of treatment, the patients should cooperate with the doctor during the treatment. Tapping with dermal needles in the affected areas and along the affected meridians may also be added to the treatment.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Wei Syndromes","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Wind Stroke","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Attack on the zang-fu organs i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Wind stirred up by upsurge of liver yang sends qi and blood upward, which together with the accumulated phlegm fire disturb the mind, leading to sudden loss of consciousness with tightly closed hands and clenched jaws, flushed face, coarse breathing, retention of urine and constipation. Excessive wind phlegm brings about ratting in the throat. A red tongue with thick yellow coating or dark grey coating, and a string-taut, rolling and forceful pulse are the signs of wind combined with phlegm fire.

[b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Severe weakness of primary qi, separation of yin and yang and exhaustion of qi in the zang organs are indicated in mouth agape, eyes closed, snoring but feeble breathing, flaccid paralysis, and incontinence of urine. Flaccid tongue and thready weak pulse suggest the deficiency of blood and prostration of the kidney yang. If complicated with cold limbs, flushed face, and fading or big floating pulse, it is a critical case, indicating exhaustion of yin in the lower portion of the body and upward rushing of the isolated yang,

[b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ There are two categories. One is that only the meridians and collaterals are attacked without the zang-fu organs being involved. The other is that after wind stroke the functions of the affected zang-fu organs have been restored, yet there exists stagnation of qi and blood in the meridians and collaterals.

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Wind phlegm enters the meridians and collaterals due to imbalance of yin and yang, or after treatment the functions of the affected zang-fu organs have been restored, but wind phlegm still blocks the meridians and collaterals, causing retarded circulation of qi and blood. This causes hemiplegia, numbness of the limbs, deviated mouth and slurring of speech. If complicated with upsurging of liver yang, and upward disturbance of wind yang, the symptoms are headache, dizziness, vertigo and twitching of muscles. If there is excessive fire in the heart and liver, there may be red eyes and flushed face, thirst, dryness of the throat and irritability. Stagnation of wind phlegm in the meridians and collaterals leads to a stringtaut and rolling pulse.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"[b]➣ a) Attack on the zang-fu organs[/b]

[b]➣ i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] As the condition is due to disturbance of the heart by phlegm fire associated with upsurging of liver yang and upward flowing of qi and blood, Baihui (GV 20) and Shuigou (GV 26) are selected to regulate qi of the Governor Vessel, effecting resuscitation, Yongquan (KI 1) is selected to conduct the heat downward, and Taichong (LR 3) to subdue the upsurging of qi in the Liver Meridian and pacify the liver yang. Pricking the twelve Jing-Well points on both hands, where qi of the three yin and three yang meridians meet, may dispel heat and restore consciousness. The spleen and stomach are the source of phlegm production. Fenglong (ST 40), the Luo (Connecting) point of the Stomach Meridian can invigorate the functions of the spleen and stomach and help to resolve the turbid phlegm. Since the Yangming Meridians of Hand and Foot supply the cheeks, Xiaguan (ST 7), Jiache (ST 6) and Hegu (LI 4) are chosen to promote the circulation of qi and blood for relieving the clenched jaws. Yamen (GV 15) and Lian- quan (CV 23), being local and adjacent points of the tongue, and Tongli (HT 5), the Luo- (Connecting) point of the Heart Meridian, may relieve stiffness of tongue.

[b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Shenque (CV 8), Qihai (CV 6) and Guanyuan (CV 4) are located on the lower abdomen along the Conception Vessel and are the main points effective for collapse. Heavy moxibustion applied on Guanyuan (CV 4), a meeting point of the Conception Vessel and three yin meridians, can strengthen the primary qi, and restore yang from collapse.

[b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Governor Vessel is the sea of all yang meridians. Baihui (GV 20), Fengfu (GV 16) combined with Tongtian (BL 7) can eliminate wind and remove obstruction from the meridians and collaterals. Since the yang meridians dominate the exterior of the body and qi, points of the yang meridians are selected to regulate qi and blood of the body and promote smooth circulation in the upper and lower portions of the body. For the upper disturbance of wind yang, Fengchi (GB 20) and Taichong (LR 3) are selected to reduce the wind and pacify the liver. Reinforcing applied to Taixi (KI 3) promotes the production of the kidney yin to nourish the liver. Reinforcing applied to Sanyinjiao (SP 6) nourishes yin and pacifies yang. For excessive fire in the heart and liver, reducing Daling (PC 7) and Xingjian (LR 2) can eliminate the fire, while reinforcing Taixi (KI 3) nourishes yin to reduce the fire. Dicang (ST 4) and Jiache (ST 6) are selected for the purpose of promoting a free circulation of qi in the meridians and collaterals around the facial region.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Wind stroke is an emergency case manifested by falling down in a fit with loss of consciousness, or hemiplegia, slurred speech and deviated mouth. It is characterized by abrupt onset with pathological changes varying quickly like the wind, from which the term "wind stroke" comes.","Patterns":"➣ Wind stroke often occurs in the aged who are in poor health, with deficiency of qi and blood or deficiency in the lower part of the body and excess in the upper part. It may be caused by deficiency of the kidney yin due to sexual indulgence, or by irregular food intake, which impedes the transportation and transformation function of the spleen, leading to production of phlegm from accumulated dampness and transformation into heat. Then there appears imbalance of yin and yang in the zang-fu organs. Other causative factors are exasperation, agitation, alcohol indulgence or overeating, over strain and stress, or invasion of exogenous pathogenic wind, all leading to upsurge of liver yang and heart fire, which makes qi and blood go upward together with turbid phlegm, disturbing the mind and resulting in this disease. In mild cases there are only symptoms showing dysfunction of the meridians and collaterals, while in severe cases both dysfunction of zang-fu organs and that of the meridians and collaterals are manifested. The syndrome indicating the attack on the zang-fu organs may be subdivided into tense (excess) type and flaccid (deficiency) type.

➣ Tense (excess) syndrome results from disturbance of the mind by the phlegm heat, or collection of excessive fire in the heart and liver, while flaccid (deficiency) syndrome results from deficiency of the primary qi or collapse of the kidney yang. In untreated or improperly treated cases, the tense syndrome tends to become flaccid and the prognosis is often poor.","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Attack on the zang-fu organs i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Falling down in a fit with loss of consciousness, tightly closed hands and clenched jaws, flushed face, coarse breathing, rattling in the throat, retention of urine, constipation, red tongue with thick yellow or dark grey coating, string-taut, rolling and forceful pulse.

[b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Falling down in a fit and sudden loss of consciousness with mouth agape and eyes closed, snoring, feeble breathing, flaccid paralysis of limbs, incontinence of urine, flaccid tongue, thready, weak pulse, and in severe cases cold limbs, or flushed face, fading or big floating pulse.

[b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ There are two categories. One is that only the meridians and collaterals are attacked without the zang-fu organs being involved. The other is that after wind stroke the functions of the affected zang-fu organs have been restored, yet there exists stagnation of qi and blood in the meridians and collaterals.

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Hemiplegia, numbness of the limbs, deviated mouth, slurring of speech, accompanied by headache, dizziness, vertigo, twitching of muscles, red eyes and flushed face, thirst, dryness of the throat, irritability, string-taut and rolling pulse.","Treatment Principle":"[b]➣ a) Attack on the zang-fu organs[/b]

[b]➣ i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Points of the Governor Vessel, the Liver Meridian of Foot-Jueyin and the twelve Jing-Well points are selected as the main points to promote resuscitation, reduce wind and fire and resolve phlegm. Either reducing method or pricking, to cause a little bleeding, is applied.

[b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Moxibustion is applied to points of the Conception Vessel to restore yang from collapse.

[b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Points along the Governor Vessel and the yang meridians of the affected side are mainly used to regulate qi and blood, remove obstruction from the meridians and collaterals and reduce the wind. Needle with even movement first from the healthy side and then the affected side.","Point Prescription":"➣[b] a) Attack on the zang-fu organs[/b]

➣ [b]➣ i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Baihui (GV 20), Shuigou (GV 26), Fenglong (ST 40), Taichong (LR 3), Yongquan (KI 1), twelve Jing-Well points on both hands (LU 11, HT 9, PC 9, LI L. TE 1, SI 1).

➣ [b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Shenque (CV 8) (indirect moxi- bustion with salt), Qihai (CV 6), Guanyuan (CV 4).

➣ [b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Baihui (GV 20), Tongtian (BL 7), Fengfu (GV 16).

➣ [b]Upper limbs:[/b] Jianyu (LI 15), Quchi (LI 11), Waiguan (TE 5), Hegu (LI 4).

➣ [b]Lower limbs:[/b] Huantiao (GB 30), Yang- lingquan (GB 34), Zusanli (ST 36), Jiexi (ST 41).","Supplementary Points":"➣[b] a) Attack on the zang-fu organs[/b]

➣ [b] i) Tense syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Supplementary Points:[/b]

➣ [b]Clenched Jaws:[/b] Xianguan (ST 7), Jiache (ST 6), Hegu (LI 14).

➣ [b]Aphasia and stiffness of tongue:[/b] Yamen (GV 15), Lianquan (CV 23), and Tongli (HT 5).

➣ [b]➣ ii) Flaccid syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]➣ b) Attack on the meridians and collaterals[/b]

➣ [b]Supplementary points:[/b]

➣ [b]Upward disturbance of wind yang:[/b] Reducing is applied to Fenchi (GB 20) and Taichong (LR 3), and reinforcing to Taixi (KI 3) and Sanyinjiao (SP 6).

➣ [b]Excessive fire in the heart and liver:[/b] Reducing is applied to Daling (PC 7) and Xingjian (LR 2), and reinforcing to Taixi (KI 3).

➣ [b]Deviated mouth:[/b] Dicang (ST 4), Jiache (ST6).","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) Wind stroke is referred as to cerebral hemorrhage, thrombosis, embolism, subarachnoid hemorrhage, etc. When the acute stage is over, there may be sequelae, such as hemiplegia, monoplegia, aphasia, etc.[/b]

[b]➣ b) Prophylactic measures for wind stroke: The aged with deficiency of qi and excessive phlegm, or with manifestations of upsurging of liver yang marked by dizziness and palpita tions, may have premonitory symptoms such as stiff tongue, slurred speech and numbness of the finger tips. Attention should be paid to diet and life style and avoid overstraining. Frequent moxibustion on Zusanli (ST 36) and Xuanzhong (GB 39) may prevent an attack of wind stroke.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Wind Stroke","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Syncope","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Dizziness, vertigo, loss of consciousness, feeble breathing are the symptoms caused by deficiency of primary qi with sudden perversion of its flow, sinking of qi in the spleen and stomach and failure of the clear yang in ascending. Cold limbs are caused by failure of yang qi to reach there. Weakness of primary qi and disabilities of vital qi in controlling the pores are shown in spontaneous sweating, and mouth agape. Deep thready pulse also suggests deficiency of vital qi.

[b]➣ b) Excess syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Perversion of qi after a fit of anger impedes the qi. Blood and qi rush upward to disturb the mind, causing sudden collapse, loss of consciousness, clenched jaws and rigid limbs. Obstruction of qi in the lung gives rise to coarse breathing. Deep and excess-type pulse is a sign of excess syndrome.

➣ Syncope manifested by sudden loss of consciousness should be distinguished from wind stroke and epilepsy.

➣ [b]Wind stroke:[/b] Loss of consciousness is complicated by hemiplegia and deviated mouth. Usually there are sequelae after restoration to consciousness.

➣ [b]Epilepsy:[/b] Loss of consciousness is accompanied by convulsions, expectoration of frothy saliva or yelling. When consciousness is regained, the patient returns to as normal.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"[b]➣ a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Shuigou (GV 26), Baihui (GV 20) and Neiguan (PC 6) are the points for resuscitation. Qihai (CV 6) and Zusanli (ST 36) are good for reinforcing qi and invigorating yang.

[b]➣ b) Excess syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Shuigou (GV 26) and Zhongchong (PC 9) are used to promote resuscitation. Hegu (LI 4) and Taichong (LR 3) are the points for regulating the circulation of qi and blood. Laogong (PC 8) and Yongquan (KI 1) promote a clear mind and smooth flow of qi and blood.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Syncope is manifested by sudden fainting, pallor, cold limbs and loss of consciousness, which often results from emotional excitement, fright, or debilitation and overstraining.","Patterns":"➣[b] a) Deficiency type: Syncope of this type is often caused by general deficiency of the primary qi and failure of clear yang in ascending due to over strain or grief and fright, or by exhaustion of qi after profuse bleeding.[/b]

➣[b] b) Excess type: It is due mainly to emotional disturbances, such as anger, fear and fright, leading to deranged flow of qi, which rushes upwards to the heart and chest, blocking the windpipe and disturbing the mind, or due to upsurging of liver yang, and upward flowing of qi followed by perversion of blood flow after a fit of anger, leading to disturbance of the mind, and resulting in loss of consciousness.[/b]","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Feeble breathing with mouth agape, spontaneous sweating, pallor, cold limbs, deep and thready pulse.

[b]➣ b) Excess syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Coarse breathing, rigid limbs, clenched jaws, deep and excess- type pulse.

➣ Syncope manifested by sudden loss of consciousness should be distinguished from wind stroke and epilepsy.

➣ [b]Wind stroke:[/b] Loss of consciousness is complicated by hemiplegia and deviated mouth. Usually there are sequelae after restoration to consciousness.

➣ [b]Epilepsy:[/b] Loss of consciousness is accompanied by convulsions, expectoration of frothy saliva or yelling. When consciousness is regained, the patient returns to as normal.","Treatment Principle":"[b]➣ a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Points of the Governor Vessel and Pericardium Meridian are selected as the main points to promote resuscitation, reinforce qi and invigorate yang. Reinforcing is applied in acupuncture, combined with moxibustion.

[b]➣ b) Excess syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Reducing is applied to points of the Governor Vessel and Pericardium Meridian to promote resuscitation and regulate the flow of qi.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b] a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Shuigou (GV 26), Baihui (GV 20), Neiguan (PC 6), Qihai (CV 6), Zusanli (ST 36).

➣ [b] b) Excess syndrome[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Shuigou (GV 26), Hegu (LI 4), Zhongchong (PV 9), Laogong (PV 8), Taichong (LR 3), Yongquan (KI 1).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b] a) Deficiency syndrome[/b]

➣ [b] b) Excess syndrome[/b]","Remarks":"➣ This condition includes simple fainting, pos- tural hypotension, hypoglycemia, hysteria, etc.","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Syncope","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Sunstroke","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Mild type[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Pathogenic summer heat tends to attack the head and gives rise to headache and dizziness. Hot skin results from the accumulation of pathogenic summer heat in the body surface. Profuse sweating, coarse breathing, dry mouth and tongue, dire thirst are all due to evaporation of body fluid by the summer heat. Superficial, large and rapid pulse is a sign showing the presence of the pathogenic summer heat.

[b]➣ b) Severe type[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] This syndrome mostly occurs in those doing physical labor in the scorching summer sun. Overfatigue plus the attack of summer heat results in lowered body resistance and excessiveness of the pathogenic factor with consumption of qi and body fluid. So there are headache, dire thirst and shortness of breath at the very beginning. The pathogenic summer heat can rapidly penetrate into the interior, affecting the pericardium and disturbing the mind, leading to loss of consciousness. Sweating and deep, forceless pulse indicate exhaustion of qi and body fluid.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"[b]➣ a) Mild type[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Dazhui (GV 14), the meeting point of the Governor Vessel and all yang meridians, Weizhong (BL 40), also named Xuexi, and Quchi (LI 11), an important point for eliminating heat, are used to dispel the summer heat. Neiguan (PC 6), the Luo-Connecting point of Jueyin Meridian of Hand, is chosen to reduce the fire and to protect the heart.

[b]➣ b) Severe type[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Summer heat is a pathogenic factor of yang nature, which is apt to attack the pericardium and disturb the mind. Shuigou (GV 26) and Baihui (GV 20) are selected to promote resuscitation. Quze (PC 6), the He-Sea point of Hand-Jueyin, Weizhong (BL 40), the He-Sea point of Foot Taiyang are pricked superficially to remove the heat from the blood. Blood letting at Shixuan (Extra) can reduce heat and promote resuscitation.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Sunstroke is an acute case occurring in summer, manifested by high fever, irritability, nausea, possibly followed by collapse and loss of consciousness. The onset of this disease is due mostly to prolonged exposure to the sun, or to a high temperature environment.","Patterns":"➣ Summer heat, a pathogenic factor, is prevalent in summer time when the weather is scorching. Long exposure to the sun and to an environment with high temperature damages qi. Invasion of pathogenic summer heat in a condition of lower resistance brings about sunstroke. Summer heat is a pathogenic factor of yang nature with a tendency to attack the human body quickly. Therefore the onset is abrupt and the changing of the pathological condition is rapid. Pathogenic summer heat is likely to damage the primary qi and consume the body fluid, leading to exhaustion of qi and yin. Furthermore, pathogenic summer heat may penetrate the pericardium and disturb the mind, leading to impairment of consciousness. Sunstroke, according to its clinical manifestations, may be classified into mild and severe types.","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Mild type[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Headache, dizziness, profuse sweating, hot skin, coarse breathing, dry mouth and tongue, dire thirst, superficial, large and rapid pulse.

[b]➣ b) Severe type[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Headache, dire thirst, and shortness of breath at first, and then collapse, loss of consciousness, sweating, deep and forceless pulse.","Treatment Principle":"[b]➣ a) Mild type[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Pericardium and Large Intestine Meridians to eliminate the summer heat.

[b]➣ b) Severe type[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel to promote resuscita tion, and to dispel the summer heat.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b] a) Mild type[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Dazhui (GV 14), Neiguan (PC 6), Quchi (LI 11), Weizhong (BL 40).

➣ [b] b) Severe type[/b]","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) This illness includes thermoplegia, ther- mospasm, heliosis, etc.[/b]

[b]➣ b) Guasha therapy: It is a popular treatment for mild sunstroke. Dip a smooth spoon into water or vegetable oil and scrape both sides of the spine, the neck, intercostal spaces, shoulder regions, cubital and axilla fossae until purplish red color appears.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM Notes":"","Important":"","Channel Full Name":"Zang-Fu Syndromes","Channel Short Name":"Sunstroke","Image ID":""},{"Short Name":"Common Cold","Full Name":"StudyGuide","Main Topic & Etiology":"","Subtopic & Manifestations":"","Analysis":"[b]➣ a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Invasion of the body surface by pathogenic wind and cold impairs the dispersing function of the lung and affects the nose, causing nasal obstruction and discharge. Pathogenic cold is of yin nature, which is likely to damage yang. Impairment of superficial yang is manifested by exterior symptoms such as chills, fever, anhidrosis, headache, even soreness and pain of the limbs. Thin white tongue coating and superficial tense pulse are the signs showing the invasion of the lung and the superficial defensive system by pathogenic wind and cold.

[b]➣ b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ [b]Analysis:[/b] Pathogenic wind heat often attacks the body through the nose and mouth. The lung is involved first. Pathogenic wind of yang nature is characterized by upward and outward dispersion. When a fight goes on between the pathogenic wind heat and the body's resistance, fever, slight aversion to wind and sweating result. When the pathogenic wind heat attacks the head, symptoms like pain and distending sensation occur in the head. In case the lung fails in dispersing and descending, there appears cough with yellow, thick sputum. When the pathogenic wind heat stifles the air passage, there is congested sore throat with thirst. Thin, white or yellowish tongue coating, and superficial, rapid pulse are the signs showing the lung and the defensive system being attacked by the pathogenic wind heat.","Treatment Method":"","Prescription":"","Explanation":"[b]➣ a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] Fengfu (GV 16) is used to relieve the exterior symptoms, eliminate wind and check headache. Fengmen (BL 12), a point of the Taiyang Meridian which dominates the surface of the whole body, is selected to regulate the circulation of qi in this meridian, to eliminate wind cold and to relieve chills and fever. As the pathogenic cold has attacked the surface of the body, and the lung is the organ related to the skin and hair, Lieque (LU 7), the Luo-Connecting point of the Lung Meridian, is used to promote the dispersing function of the lung and to check cough. Fengchi (GB 20), a point at the intersection of the Foot Shaoyang and Yang Linking Vessels, of which the latter dominates yang and the exterior, is used to eliminate wind cold. Since the Taiyin and Yangming Meridians are externally and internally related, Hegu (LI 4), the Yuan-Primary point of the Yangming Meridian, is used to eliminate the pathogenic factors and relieve the exterior symptoms.

[b]➣ b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Shaoyang and Yangming Meridians to eliminate wind heat.

➣ [b]Explanation:[/b] The Governor Vessel is the sea of all the yang meridians. Dazhui (GV 14), a point where all the yang meridians meet, is used to eliminate heat and other pathogenic factors of yang nature. Hegu (LI 4) and Quchi (LI 11) are the Yuan-Primary point and the He- Sea point of the Yangming Meridian of Hand respectively. Since the Yangming and Taiyin Meridians of Hand are externally and internally related, reducing applied to these two points can clear lung qi and reduce heat. Yuji (LU 10), the Ying-Spring point of the Lung Meridian, in combination with Shaoshang (LU 11), can eliminate heat from the lung and ease the throat. Waiguan (TE 5), the Luo-Connecting point of the Shaoyang Meridian of Hand connecting with the Yang Linking Vessel, can dispel pathogenic factors of a yang nature in the exterior of the body and eliminate heat.","Supplementary & Remarks":"","Combinations":"","Description":"➣ Common cold is an exogenous ailment with headache, nasal obstruction, aversion to wind and fever as its main manifestations. It often results from lowered superficial resistance and invasion of the exogenous pathogenic factors. It may occur in any season. [b]According to the difference in weather, pathogenic factors and body constitution, the manifestations can be classified into two types:[/b] wind cold and wind heat.","Patterns":"➣ This disease is often due to delicate constitution and weakened body resistance which makes the body inadaptable to intense changes of the weather such as abnormal cold or warmth. Then the exogenous pathogenic wind invades the body through the pores, skin, mouth and nose, leading to manifestations related to lung and the defensive function. Very often pathogenic wind combined with other pathogenic factors like pathogenic cold causes a wind cold syndrome, or with pathogenic heat causes a wind heat syndrome. Invasion of exogenous pathogenic wind and cold may retard the lung's dispersing function and block the pores, while invasion of exogenous pathogenic wind and heat may impair the lung's descending function by the evaporating heat, and lead to abnormal functioning of the pores. In addition, since the patient's body builds are different and internal and external pathogenic factors affect individuals differently, the manifestations of the invasions will be varied. For patients with yang deficiency, wind cold syndrome is mostly seen, while for those with yin deficiency, wind heat syndrome is often found.","Diagnosis & Differentiation":"[b]➣ a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Chills, fever, anhidro- sis, headache, soreness and pain of the limbs, nasal obstruction, running nose, itching of the throat, cough, hoarse voice, profuse thin sputum, thin white tongue coating, superficial and tense pulse.

[b]➣ b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ [b]Main manifestations:[/b] Fever, sweating, slight aversion to wind, pain and distending sensation of the head, cough with yellow, thick sputum, congested and sore throat, thirst, thin white or yellowish tongue coating, superficial and rapid pulse.","Treatment Principle":"[b]➣ a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b]Method:[/b] Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Taiyang and Shaoyang Meridians to eliminate wind cold and relieve exterior symptoms. Even movement combined with moxibustion is applied to patients with weakened constitutions.

[b]➣ b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Shaoyang and Yangming Meridians to eliminate wind heat.","Point Prescription":"➣ [b] a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Fengfu (GV 16), Fengmen (BL 12), Fengchi (GB 20), Lieque (LU 7), Hegu (LI 4).

➣ [b] b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Shaoyang and Yangming Meridians to eliminate wind heat.

➣ [b]Prescription:[/b] Dazhui (GV 14), Quchi (LI 11), Waiguan (TE 5), Hegu (LI 4), Yuji (LU 10), Shaoshang (LU 11).","Supplementary Points":"➣ [b] a) Wind cold[/b]

➣ [b] b) Wind heat[/b]

➣ Reducing is applied to the points of the Governor Vessel, Shaoyang and Yangming Meridians to eliminate wind heat.","Remarks":"[b]➣ a) The above treatment can also be used[/b]

➣ for other viral and bacterial infections of the upper respiratory tract as well as influenza. However, common cold should be distinguished from other infectious diseases with similar symptoms at the early stage.

[b]➣ b) Prophylactic measures: Moxibustion is applied daily to Fengmen (BL 12) or Zusanli (ST 36) to prevent common cold during its season.[/b]","Flashcard View":"","Notes":"","CAM
